THE LAW OF MOORE, FORMULATED BY INTEL CO-FOUNDER GORDY MOORE IN 1965, PREDICTS THAT THE NUMBER OF TRANSISTORS ON A MICROPROCESSOR WILL DOUBLE EVERY TWO YEARS. THIS TRENDS HAS DRIVEN THE RAPID ADVANCEMENT OF COMPUTING TECHNOLOGY FOR DECADES, LEADING TO INCREASED PROCESSING POWER, SMALLER DEVICES, AND LOWER COSTS. HOWEVER, AS TECHNOLOGY HAS PROGRESSED, PHYSICAL LIMITATIONS HAVE BECOME MORE PREVALENT, SUCH AS HEAT DISSIPATION, QUANTUM TUNNELLING, AND THE MINIMUM SIZE OF TRANSISTORS. THESE FACTORS HAVE MADE IT CHALLENGING TO CONTINUE THE SAME RATE OF GROWTH, LEADING TO SPECULATION ABOUT WHEN THE LAW OF MOORE WILL CEASE TO BE VALID.

***  
THE EXACT TIME WHEN THE LAW OF MOORE WILL STOP IS DIFFICULT TO PREDICT, AS IT DEPENDS ON ADVANCES IN MATERIALS SCIENCE, MANUFACTURING TECHNIQUES, AND NEW COMPUTING ARCHITECTURES. SOME EXPERTS BELIEVE THAT THE LAW MAY STILL HOLD FOR A FEW MORE YEARS, WHILE OTHERS ARGUE THAT IT HAS ALREADY REACHED ITS LIMITS. INSTEAD OF A Sudden END, THE LAW MAY EVOLVE INTO A NEW FORM OF PROGRESSION, SUCH AS THREE-DIMENSIONAL CHIPS OR QUANTUM COMPUTING. THE FUTURE OF COMPUTING WILL LIKELY INVOLVE INNOVATIONS THAT GO BEYOND THE SIMPLE DOUBLING OF TRANSISTORS, CHANGING HOW WE THINK ABOUT TECHNOLOGICAL PROGRESS.
